title: From Vector Space to Probabilistic Ranking

slides.title: Recap: What We Know
slides.content:
Vector Space Model (Lecture 1)

  • Documents and queries as vectors in term space
  • Similarity measured by cosine distance
  • Foundation for understanding term-based retrieval

Indexing (Lecture 2)

  • Inverted index: term $\to$ posting list (docID, tf, positions)
  • Accumulator-based scoring: only touch documents with term overlap
  • Dense indexing: IVF+PQ for semantic vectors

Today's Question

We can store and retrieve efficiently. But how do we score?

slides.title: The Probability Ranking Principle (PRP)
slides.content:
Theorem (Robertson, 1977)

If documents are ranked by decreasing probability of relevance given the query, then the expected effectiveness of the system is maximized---under the assumption of independent relevance.
Formally: Rank by $P(\text{relevant} \mid q, d)$ Implications:
  • Gives a theoretical justification for probabilistic scoring
  • All scoring functions (TF-IDF, BM25, LM) are approximations of this ideal
  • Independence assumption is strong---violated in practice (documents are correlated)

Key Insight

The PRP tells us to optimize. BM25 and Language Models tell us .

slides.title: The Ranking Problem
slides.content:
Formal Definition

Given:
  • Query $q$ (sequence of terms)
  • Document collection $D$
  • Inverted index (from Lecture 2)
Return: Ranked list scored by relevance $P(\text{relevant}|q,d)$

slides.title: Term Weighting: TF-IDF
slides.left_content:
Term Frequency (TF)
  • More occurrences $\rightarrow$ more important
  • $\text{tf}(t,d) = \text{count}(t,d)$
Inverse Document Frequency (IDF)
  • Rare terms $\rightarrow$ more discriminative
  • $\text{idf}(t) = \log\frac{N}{\text{df}_t}$

slides.title: TF-IDF Example
slides.content:
Query: ``information retrieval'' ($N = 100{,}000$ documents)
TermDoc 1Doc 2dfIDFWeight
information3150001.30High TF
retrieval205002.30High IDF
the20150500000.30Low IDF

Problem

Linear TF growth unrealistic:
  • 100 occurrences $\neq$ 100$\times$ more relevant than 1
  • Need saturation

slides.title: Motivation: TF Saturation
slides.content:
Observation:
  • Relevance increases with TF
  • But saturates quickly
Example:
  • ``algorithm'' 5$\times$ vs. 50$\times$
  • 5$\rightarrow$10 is meaningful
  • 45$\rightarrow$50 barely noticeable

Need

Non-linear TF component that saturates gracefully

slides.title: Motivation: TF Saturation (Figure)
BM25 Formula
slides.content:
Complete Formula

$$\text{BM25}(q,d) = \sum_{t \in q} \text{IDF}(t) \cdot \frac{f(t,d) \cdot (k_1+1)}{f(t,d) + k_1 \cdot \left(1-b+b \cdot \frac{|d|}{\text{avgdl}}\right)}$$
Three components:
  1. IDF: Term importance (like TF-IDF)
  2. Saturating TF: Non-linear term frequency
  3. Length normalization: Penalize long documents
Standard parameters: $k_1 = 1.2$, $b = 0.75$

slides.title: BM25 Component 1: IDF
slides.content:
IDF Formula

$$\text{IDF}(t) = \log\frac{N - \text{df}_t + 0.5}{\text{df}_t + 0.5}$$
where:
  • $N$ = total number of documents
  • $\text{df}_t$ = document frequency of term $t$
Properties:
  • Similar to TF-IDF but probabilistically motivated
  • Can be negative for very common terms ($\text{df} > N/2$)
  • Rare terms get high positive IDF
  • Common terms get low or negative IDF

slides.title: BM25 Component 2: Saturating TF
slides.content:
Saturating TF Formula

$$\frac{f(t,d) \cdot (k_1+1)}{f(t,d) + k_1}$$
Parameter $k_1$ (typically 1.2--2.0):
  • $k_1 \to 0$: Boolean matching (TF doesn't matter)
  • $k_1 \to \infty$: Linear TF (like TF-IDF)
  • $k_1 = 1.2$: Moderate saturation (standard)
TF$k_1=1.2$$k_1=2.0$
10.550.67
51.531.67
101.791.83
501.981.96

slides.title: BM25 Component 2: Saturating TF (Figure)
BM25 Component 3: Length Normalization
slides.content:
Length Normalization Term

$$1 - b + b \cdot \frac{|d|}{\text{avgdl}}$$
Parameter $b$ (typically 0.75):
  • $b = 0$: No length normalization
  • $b = 1$: Full normalization (penalize long docs heavily)
  • $b = 0.75$: Partial normalization (standard)
Why needed:
  • Long documents have more terms just by chance
  • Should we penalize or not? Depends on collection
  • $b$ controls the trade-off

slides.title: BM25 Example: Effect of Document Length
BM25 Example: Rare vs. Common Terms
slides.content:
Collection: $N = 100{,}000$ documents
TermdfIDFInterpretation
quantum1006.21Very discriminative
algorithm5{,}0002.99Moderately discriminative
system50{,}000$-0.41$Not discriminative
the99{,}000$-6.21$Actively harmful

Key Insight

IDF difference drives discrimination: Rare terms dominate scoring (Matches on ``quantum'' worth $\sim$15$\times$ matches on ``system''). Common terms contribute little or negatively

slides.title: A Different Ranking Philosophy
slides.left_content:
BM25 Approach:
  • ``How well does document match query?''
  • Scoring based on term overlap
  • Weighted by importance (IDF, TF)

Matching Score

$$\text{score}(q,d) = \sum_{t} w(t,q,d)$$
slides.right_content:
Language Model Approach:
  • ``How likely would this document generate this query?''
  • Rank by $P(q|d)$
  • Probabilistic generative model

Generative Probability

$$\text{score}(q,d) = P(q|d)$$

slides.title: A Different Ranking Philosophy
slides.left_content:
BM25 Approach:
  • ``How well does document match query?''
  • Scoring based on term overlap
  • Weighted by importance (IDF, TF)
slides.right_content:
Language Model Approach:
  • ``How likely would this document generate this query?''
  • Rank by $P(q|d)$
  • Probabilistic generative model

slides.title: The Generative Story
slides.content:
Intuition

Imagine randomly sampling words from a document. The document that is most likely to produce your query terms is ranked highest.
How it works step by step:
  1. Pick a document $d$ from the collection
  2. The document defines a language model --- a probability distribution $P(w|d)$ over words
  3. Sample query words from this distribution: $P(q|d) = \prod_{w \in q} P(w|d)$
  4. Rank documents by $P(q|d)$ --- those more likely to ``emit'' the query rank higher

slides.title: Visualizing Language Models
Query Likelihood Model: Philosophy
slides.content:
The Generative Story

Instead of matching keywords, we ask: ``What is the probability that this document would produce this query?''
Mathematical Formulation:
  • Assume query terms $w \in q$ are sampled independently from the document $d$ (unigram assumption).
  • Rank documents by: $$P(q|d) = \prod_{w \in q} P(w|d)$$
  • In practice, we use log-probabilities to avoid underflow: $$\text{score}(q,d) = \sum_{w \in q} \log P(w|d)$$

slides.title: Estimation
slides.content:
Maximum Likelihood Estimate (MLE)

How do we calculate $P(w|d)$? The simplest way is term frequency: $$P_{\text{ML}}(w|d) = \frac{\text{count}(w,d)}{|d|}$$
But what is the catch here?

The Catch

If a query term $w$ is not in the document, then $P_{\text{ML}}(w|d) = 0$.
  • The product $P(q|d) = 0$ for the entire query!
  • One missing term kills the score, even for a 90% relevant document.
Example: Query ``machine learning algorithms'' misses a document about ``machine learning'' because the word ``algorithms'' is absent.

slides.title: Zero Probability Example
slides.content:
Query: ``machine learning algorithms'' Document: ``This paper presents a novel neural network approach to supervised machine learning.'' (15 words)
Termcount(w,d)$P_{\text{ML}}(w|d)$Result
machine1$1/15 = 0.067$✓
learning1$1/15 = 0.067$✓
algorithms0$0/15 = 0$ZERO!
$P(q|d)$

Problem

Document is clearly relevant, but one missing term kills the entire score!

slides.title: Smoothing: Core Idea
slides.content:
Principle

Mix document evidence with collection evidence: $$P(w|d) = f(\text{document evidence}, \text{collection evidence})$$
Collection Language Model: $$P(w|C) = \frac{\sum_{d' \in C} \text{count}(w,d')}{\sum_{d' \in C} |d'|}$$

Effect

Even if $\text{count}(w,d) = 0$, we have $P(w|d) > 0$ from collection

slides.title: Jelinek-Mercer Smoothing
slides.content:
Formula

$$P(w|d) = (1-\lambda) \cdot P_{\text{ML}}(w|d) + \lambda \cdot P(w|C)$$
Parameter $\lambda$ (typically 0.1--0.3):
  • $\lambda = 0$: Only use document (no smoothing)
  • $\lambda = 1$: Only use collection (ignore document)
  • $\lambda = 0.1$: Mostly trust document, small collection backup
Properties:
  • ✓ Simple and interpretable
  • ✓ Same $\lambda$ for all documents
  • Doesn't adapt to document length

slides.title: Dirichlet Smoothing
slides.content:
Formula

$$P(w|d) = \frac{\text{count}(w,d) + \mu \cdot P(w|C)}{|d| + \mu}$$
Parameter $\mu$ (typically 1000--2500):
  • Smoothing strength (number of ``pseudo-counts'' from collection)
  • Higher $\mu$: More smoothing. Lower $\mu$: Less smoothing.
Key Difference: Length-Adaptive
  • Short documents: $\mu$ dominates denominator $\rightarrow$ rely more on collection
  • Long documents: Actual counts dominate $\rightarrow$ rely on document
  • Automatically adapts smoothing to document length!

slides.title: Dirichlet Smoothing: Length Adaptation Example
slides.content:
Term ``algorithm'' with $P(w|C) = 0.001$, $\mu = 2000$
Documentcount(w,d)$|d|$$P(w|d)$
Short doc2100$\frac{2 + 2}{2100} = 0.0019$
Long doc22000$\frac{2 + 2}{4000} = 0.001$

Observation

  • Same term count (2), but short doc $\to$ higher probability
  • Long doc $\to$ more smoothing, closer to collection probability
  • This is automatic based on $|d|$

slides.title: Query Likelihood Scoring: Step by Step
slides.content:
The Recipe

Given query $q$ and document $d$, score = $\log P(q|d)$:
  1. Estimate via MLE: $\displaystyle P_{\text{ML}}(w|d) = \frac{\text{count}(w,d)}{|d|}$
  2. Smooth (JM): $\hat{P}(w|d) = (1-\lambda)\,P_{\text{ML}}(w|d) + \lambda\,P(w|C)$
  3. Score: $\displaystyle\text{score}(q,d) = \sum_{w \in q} \log \hat{P}(w|d)$
  4. Rank by score --- higher score = more relevant.

title: Query Expansion and Pseudo Relevance Feedback

slides.title: The Vocabulary Mismatch Problem
slides.right_content:
General Domain
  • Query: ``elderly care''
  • Document: ``geriatric nursing''
Result
  • BM25 score = 0
  • Relevant doc not retrieved

slides.title: Relevance Feedback Overview
slides.content:
Explicit Relevance Feedback

User marks documents as relevant/non-relevant
  • Impractical: users won't give feedback
  • Chicken-and-egg: need good initial results

Pseudo Relevance Feedback (PRF)

Assumption: Top-$k$ retrieved documents are relevant
  • ✓ No user interaction needed
  • ✓ Automatic query expansion
  • Risk: If assumption fails $\rightarrow$ query drift
Process:
  1. Retrieve initial results with original query
  2. Extract terms from top-$k$ documents
  3. Expand query with these terms
  4. Re-retrieve with expanded query

slides.title: RM3: Relevance Model 3
slides.content:
Provides a probabilistic way to perform query expansion by building a model of relevance ($R$) from the initial top-$k$ results. Phase 1: Estimating the Relevance Model $P(w|R)$ We want to find terms $w$ that are likely to appear in relevant documents: $$P(w|R) \approx \sum_{d \in \text{top-}k} \underbrace{P(w|d)}_{\text{term presence}} \cdot \underbrace{P(d|q)}_{\text{doc relevance weight}}$$
  • $P(w|d)$ is the smoothed language model of document $d$.
  • $P(d|q)$ is the score of the document from the first retrieval stage.
  • Terms common across many high-scoring documents get the highest $P(w|R)$.

slides.title: RM3: Interpolation and Final Query
slides.content:
Phase 2: Combining with the Original Query To prevent the query from drifting too far, we interpolate the relevance model with the original query's word

distribution:

$$P(w|q_{\text{new}}) = (1-\lambda) P(w|q_{\text{orig}}) + \lambda P(w|R)$$
  • $\lambda=1$: Full query expansion (total trust in top-$k$).
  • $\lambda=0$: No expansion (original query only).
  • Common default: $\lambda \approx 0.5$.

The Final Retrieval

The top fb_terms from $P(w|q_{\text{new}})$ are used to form a new weighted query for the second stage.

slides.title: RM3: Key Parameters
slides.content:
fb_docs (Feedback Documents): 3--10

How many top documents to use for expansion
  • Too few: May miss relevant terms. Too many: Risk non-relevant docs.
  • Standard: 10

fb_terms (Feedback Terms): 10--20

How many expansion terms to add
  • Too few: Limited benefit. Too many: Noise.
  • Standard: 10

$\lambda$ (Interpolation Weight): 0.5--0.8

Original vs. expansion weight
  • Lower (0.5): Trust original query more
  • Higher (0.8): Trust expansion more

slides.title: RM3 Example
slides.content:
Original Query: ``machine learning'' Top-3 Retrieved Documents (fb_docs=3):
  1. ``Neural networks and deep learning algorithms...''
  2. ``Supervised machine learning techniques...''
  3. ``Machine learning applications in AI...''
Extracted Terms (fb_terms=5):
  • neural (P=0.15), deep (P=0.12), supervised (P=0.10), algorithms (P=0.09), AI (P=0.08)
Expanded Query ($\lambda=0.6$): ``machine learning neural deep supervised algorithms AI''

slides.title: Beyond PRF: Modern Query Expansion Strategies
slides.content:
Key Insight

LLMs have parametric knowledge --- they can generate expansion terms without retrieving any documents first. No risk of PRF's ``bad top-$k$'' problem!

slides.title: Beyond PRF: Modern Query Expansion Strategies (Figure)
Strategy 1: LLM Synonym & Paraphrase Generation --- Intuition
slides.content:
Query: ``heart attack symptoms'' $\longrightarrow$ BM25 score = 0 for a document that says ``myocardial infarction''

Core Idea

LLMs were trained on billions of words --- they that doctors call a heart attack a ``myocardial infarction''. Ask the LLM to bridge the vocabulary gap before retrieval runs.

Key Advantage over PRF

No documents needed first --- LLM's parametric knowledge bridges the gap from the start.

slides.title: Strategy 1: LLM Synonym & Paraphrase Generation --- Intuition (Figure)
Strategy 1: LLM Synonym & Paraphrase Generation --- Method
slides.content:
Prompt:

Box

Given the search query: "heart attack symptoms" Generate 5 alternative phrasings and medical synonyms that a relevant document might use. Return only the terms, one per line.
LLM Output:
  • myocardial infarction signs
  • cardiac arrest warning indicators
  • chest pain emergency symptoms
  • coronary event presentation
  • acute heart failure signs
Expanded BM25 query: ``heart attack symptoms myocardial infarction cardiac coronary chest pain''

Advantage

Bridges the exact vocabulary gap that BM25 cannot handle on its own

slides.title: Strategy 2: Hypothetical Document Generation (HyDE)
slides.content:
The Insight

BM25 needs {term overlap} between query and document. A 3-word query has very little overlap. A hypothetical relevant document would have much more overlap with real documents.

Core Idea (Gao et al., 2022)

``What if we generated the answer first, then searched for it?''

slides.title: Strategy 2: Hypothetical Document Generation (HyDE)
slides.content:
Core Idea (Gao et al., 2023)

``What if we generated the answer first, then searched for it?''

slides.title: Strategy 2: Hypothetical Document Generation (HyDE) (Figure)
Strategy 2: Hypothetical Document Generation (HyDE)
slides.content:
Prompt:

Box

Write a short paragraph that would be a perfect answer to the question: "What causes ocean acidification?"
LLM Output: ``Ocean acidification is primarily caused by the absorption of atmospheric CO$_2$ by seawater. When CO$_2$ dissolves, it forms carbonic acid, which lowers the pH of the ocean. This process threatens marine organisms with calcium carbonate shells, such as corals and mollusks...'' Sparse path: Extract key terms $\to$ ``carbonic acid'', ``calcium carbonate'', ``pH'', ``seawater'', ``corals'' $\to$ BM25 query Dense path: Embed the hypothetical doc $\to$ use as ANN query vector (Lecture 5)

slides.title: Strategy 2: Hypothetical Document Generation (HyDE)
slides.left_content:
✓ When it works well:
  • Factual / informational queries
  • Domain-specific terminology
slides.right_content:
Risk:
  • LLM hallucinations pollute terms
  • Adds LLM latency per query

slides.title: Strategy 3: Chain-of-Thought Query Decomposition --- Intuition
slides.content:
The Problem

``How did the 2008 financial crisis affect European housing markets?'' No single document covers all aspects. BM25 on the full query retrieves almost nothing.

Core Idea

Decompose complex queries into atomic sub-queries, retrieve evidence for each independently, then merge the results. More recall, less vocabulary mismatch.

slides.title: Strategy 3: Chain-of-Thought Query Decomposition --- Intuition
slides.content:
Core Idea

Decompose complex queries into atomic sub-queries, retrieve evidence for each independently, then merge the results. More recall, less vocabulary mismatch.

slides.title: Strategy 3: Chain-of-Thought Query Decomposition --- Intuition (Figure)
Strategy 3: Chain-of-Thought Query Decomposition --- Method
slides.content:
Prompt:

Box

I need to answer: "How did the 2008 financial crisis affect European housing markets?" Break this into 3-4 simpler search queries that together would cover the full answer.
LLM Output:
  1. ``2008 financial crisis causes subprime mortgage''
  2. ``European housing prices 2008 2009 decline''
  3. ``EU bank bailouts housing market impact''
  4. ``European mortgage lending regulations post-crisis''

Pipeline

BM25$_1$ $\parallel$ BM25$_2$ $\parallel$ BM25$_3$ $\parallel$ BM25$_4$ $\to$ merge (union / RRF) $\to$ better recall than single complex query

Best for

Multi-hop reasoning queries, complex informational needs. Not for simple keyword lookups.

slides.title: Strategy 4: GenQR (Generative Query Reformulation) --- Intuition
slides.content:
The Problem with ``term lists'' expansions

Synonyms/HyDE/Query2Doc can help, but often produce {bag-of-terms} or long pseudo-text. Sometimes you just want a better query that expresses the same intent.

Core Idea (Wang et al., 2022)

Use an LLM to rewrite the query itself into 3--5 high-quality paraphrases, then retrieve using the reformulated query (often appended/interpolated with the original).

Key point vs PRF

No dependence on top-$k$ pseudo-relevant docs: GenQR is direct reformulation using only $q_0$.

slides.title: Strategy 4: GenQR (Generative Query Reformulation) --- Intuition (Figure)
Comparison: Classical vs. LLM Query Expansion
slides.content:
AspectClassical (RM3)LLM-Based
Knowledge sourceTop-$k$ retrieved docsParametric (training data)
Failure modeQuery drift (bad top-$k$)Hallucination
LatencyFast (stats only)Slower (LLM inference)
CostFreeAPI cost / GPU
Domain knowledgeNeeds in-domain corpusPre-trained, zero-shot
Cold-startFails (empty index)Works (no index needed)
InterpretabilityTerms from real docsGenerated terms

Best Practice (2024+)

Combine both: Use LLM expansion to improve the initial retrieval, then apply RM3 on the (now better) top-$k$ results. The two methods are complementary.

slides.title: When Query Expansion Helps vs. Hurts
slides.left_content:
✓ Helps When:

  • Initial results mostly relevant
  • Vocabulary mismatch present
  • Broad informational queries
  • Query: ``climate change''
  • Expansion adds: ``warming'', ``CO2'', ``emissions''
slides.right_content:
{55

Hurts When:}
  • Initial results off-topic
  • Ambiguous queries
  • Very specific queries
  • Query: ``jaguar''
  • Expansion adds: ``car'', ``speed'' (wrong sense!)

slides.title: Query Drift
slides.content:
Query Drift Example

Query: ``python programming'' Top result: Article about Python snake species RM3 expansion: ``reptile'', ``snake'', ``habitat'', ``venom'' LLM expansion: ``programming language'', ``syntax'', ``libraries'' ✓ Correct!

slides.title: What We Covered Today
slides.content:
Sparse, Term-Based Ranking

  • BM25: Probabilistic relevance, saturating TF, length normalization
  • Language Models: Generative probabilities, smoothing essential
  • Query Expansion: RM3 for vocabulary mismatch + LLM-based alternatives

Fundamental Limitations

  • Still bag-of-words (order doesn't matter)
  • Exact term matching only (even with expansion)
  • No semantic understanding
  • ``car'' and ``automobile'' completely different
  • ``bank'' (river) vs. ``bank'' (financial) treated identically

slides.title: Appendix: BM25 Worked Example (cont.)
slides.content:
2. Final Scores ($k_1=1.2, b=0.75$):
StepDoc A ($|d|=300$)Doc B ($|d|=800$)
Length Norm$1 - 0.75 + 0.75(\frac{300}{500}) = \mathbf{0.7}$$1 - 0.75 + 0.75(\frac{800}{500}) = \mathbf{1.45}$
TF(q) term$\frac{5 \times 2.2}{5 + 1.2 \times 0.7} = \mathbf{1.88}$$\frac{8 \times 2.2}{8 + 1.2 \times 1.45} = \mathbf{1.81}$
TF(a) term$\frac{3 \times 2.2}{3 + 1.2 \times 0.7} = \mathbf{1.72}$$\frac{1 \times 2.2}{1 + 1.2 \times 1.45} = \mathbf{0.80}$
Total Score$6.21(1.88) + 2.94(1.72) = \mathbf{16.73}$$6.21(1.81) + 2.94(0.80) = \mathbf{13.59}$

Insight

Doc A wins because it is concise and covers both query terms effectively. Doc B is penalized for its length and lack of the second term (algorithm).
